question: What is the box model in CSS? Which CSS properties are part of it?
answer:
Every HTML element is wrapped in a rectangular box. The box model is what the browser uses to determine the height and width of that box. The CSS box consists of width and height (or default values plus the content inside), padding, borders, and margin.

Box model in CSS

Content: the actual content of the box, where the text or image is placed.
Padding: the area surrounding the content (the space between the content and the border).
Border: the area surrounding the padding.
Margin: the area surrounding the border.

question: What are the advantages of using CSS?
answer:
The main advantages of CSS are:

Separation of content from presentation - CSS lets you present the same content in different formats across mobile, desktop, or laptop.
Easy to maintain - a well-structured CSS file can change the look and feel of an entire site through small edits. A single style change can update every element on every page automatically.
Bandwidth - when used effectively, style sheets are cached by the browser and reused across pages without being re-downloaded.

question: What are the limitations of CSS?
answer:
The main disadvantages of CSS are:

Browser compatibility: some selectors are supported in certain browsers and not others. You often have to check support using the @supports rule.
Cross-browser issues: some selectors behave differently across browsers.
No parent selector: there is currently no native way in CSS to select a parent element based on its children.

question: How do you include CSS in a webpage?
answer: There are several ways to include CSS in a webpage: 1. External style sheet - an external file linked to your HTML document. Use the tag to attach the style sheet to the page: 2. Embedded CSS with a Write the rules between the opening and closing

question: What are the different types of selectors in CSS?
What is a CSS preprocessor? What are Sass, Less, and Stylus?
What are vh and vw (viewport height and viewport width) in CSS?
answer: vh and vw are CSS units used to size elements as a percentage of the viewport, and are mainly used in responsive design. 1vh equals 1/100 of the viewport's height; 1vw equals 1/100 of the viewport's width. For example, if the browser is 1000px tall, 1vh equals 10px; if it is 1000px wide, 1vw equals 10px.

question: What is the difference between reset CSS and normalize CSS?
answer:
Reset CSS: a CSS reset aims to remove all built-in browser styling. For example, the margins, paddings, and font sizes of all elements are reset to the same baseline.

Normalize CSS: a normalize stylesheet aims to make built-in browser styling consistent across browsers. It also corrects bugs and common browser inconsistencies while preserving useful defaults.

question: What is the difference between inline, inline-block, and block?
answer:
Block elements: block elements always start on a new line and take up the full available width of their container. Examples include
and

. Inline elements: inline elements do not start on a new line; they sit on the same line as the surrounding content and other inline tags. Examples include , , , and . Inline-block elements: inline-block elements flow with surrounding content like inline elements, but, unlike inline elements, they accept padding and margin on all four sides as well as explicit width and height.

question: How do you test a webpage in different browsers?
answer: It is most important to test a website in different browsers when you first design it or when you make major changes. It is also important to repeat these tests periodically, since browsers receive frequent updates that can change their rendering behavior.

question: What is a pseudo-element? What is a pseudo-class?
How do you specify units in CSS? What are the different options?
answer:
There are several ways to specify units in CSS, including px, em, pt, and percentage (%).

px (pixel) gives fine-grained control and maintains alignment, because 1px or a whole multiple of 1px is guaranteed to look sharp. Pixels do not cascade.

em maintains relative size and enables responsive fonts. em values cascade: 1em equals the current font-size of the element (or the browser default if none is set). If you set the font-size to 16px, then 1em equals 16px. A common practice is to set the body's font-size to 62.5% (equal to 10px) so that 1em equals 10px.

pt (point) is traditionally used in print. 1pt equals 1/72 inch, and it is a fixed-size unit.

% (percentage) sets the font-size relative to the parent element's font-size, so you need to set a reasonable base font-size on the body.

question: Do margin-top and margin-bottom affect inline elements?
answer: No. margin-top and margin-bottom have no effect on inline elements, because inline elements flow with the surrounding text content rather than occupying a vertical block of their own.

question: Explain the CSS position property.
answer:
Absolute

Use position: absolute to place an element exactly where you want it. The element is removed from the normal flow and positioned relative to its nearest positioned ancestor. If no positioned ancestor exists, it falls back to the initial containing block (effectively the page).

Relative

position: relative is "relative to itself". Setting position: relative on an element without any offset values has no visual effect on its position. It does, however, enable z-index on the element and establishes it as the containing block for any absolutely positioned children, which will then be positioned relative to it.

Fixed

position: fixed positions the element relative to the viewport (the browser window). The viewport does not change when you scroll, so a fixed element stays in the same place on screen.

Static

position: static is the default for every page element. The only reason you would ever set position: static explicitly is to forcefully undo positioning that was applied to an element outside of your control.

Sticky

Sticky positioning is a hybrid of relative and fixed. The element is treated as relatively positioned until it crosses a specified scroll threshold, at which point it is treated as fixed.

question: When does DOM reflow occur?
answer:
Reflow is the browser's process of re-calculating the positions and geometries of elements in the document in order to re-render part or all of the page.

Reflow occurs when you:

Insert, remove, or update an element in the DOM.
Modify content on the page, e.g. the text in an input box.
Move a DOM element.
Animate a DOM element.
Take measurements of an element such as offsetHeight or getComputedStyle.
Change a CSS style that affects layout.

question: What are the different box-sizing values?
answer:
The box-sizing CSS property controls how the total width and height of an element are calculated.

content-box: the default. The width and height values apply only to the element's content. Padding and border are added outside of those dimensions.

padding-box: the width and height values include the content and the padding. The border is added outside of those dimensions. This value was only ever supported by Firefox and has since been removed from the spec.

border-box: the width and height values include the content, padding, and border.

question: How do you center-align a div inside another div?
Can you name the four @media types?
answer:
The four @media types are:

all - the default; targets all media-type devices.
screen - targets computer and mobile screens.
print - targets printers and print previews.
speech - targets screen readers and other speech synthesizers.

question: What is the CSS grid system?
answer: CSS Grid Layout is the most powerful layout system available in CSS. It is a two-dimensional system, meaning it can handle both columns and rows at the same time, unlike flexbox, which is largely one-dimensional.

question: What are the different ways to hide an element using CSS?
answer:
display: none removes the element from the layout entirely. It is not exposed to screen readers, and although the element still exists in the DOM, it is not rendered.

visibility: hidden hides the element visually but keeps its space in the layout. The element is still in the DOM and is generally exposed to screen readers.

position: absolute with off-screen coordinates renders the element outside the visible viewport, so it is invisible to users but may still be accessible to assistive technology.

question: What does the :root pseudo-class refer to?
answer: The :root pseudo-class targets the highest-level (root) element in the document tree - in an HTML document, this is the <html> element. It is defined in the CSS Selectors Level 3 specification and is often used to declare CSS custom properties (variables) that should be available throughout the document.

question: What does accessibility (a11y) mean?
answer:
Accessibility refers to designing software and hardware so that systems can be used by people with disabilities, such as visual impairment, hearing impairment, or limited motor function.

For example, a website built with accessibility in mind might offer text-to-speech support, keyboard navigation, and sufficient color contrast. In the U.S., public-sector websites are required to meet accessibility compliance under Section 508, which provides guidelines and best practices for the key areas of accessibility that all users should be able to rely on.

question: How do you restore the default value of a CSS property?
answer: The initial keyword can be used to reset a property to its default value as defined by the CSS specification.

question: What is the difference between CSS grid and flexbox?
answer:
CSS Grid Layout is a two-dimensional system, meaning it handles both columns and rows. Grid is intended for larger-scale layouts that are not strictly linear in design.

Flexbox is largely a one-dimensional system, laying items out in either a row or a column. Flexbox is most appropriate for the individual components of an application.

question: How does calc() work?
What are CSS custom properties (CSS variables)?
What is the difference between CSS variables and preprocessor (Sass, Less, Stylus) variables?
answer:
CSS variables can be used without a preprocessor; they are supported by all modern browsers.

CSS variables cascade through the DOM and can be redefined per scope. Preprocessor variables, by contrast, are resolved at compile time and do not cascade.

CSS variables can also be read and updated at runtime from JavaScript, which is not possible with preprocessor variables.

question: What does * { box-sizing: border-box; } do? What are its advantages?
answer:
It makes every element in the document include its padding and border within the element's declared width and height, rather than adding them on top.

With box-sizing: border-box, the height of an element is calculated as: content height + vertical padding + vertical border width.

The width of an element is calculated as: content width + horizontal padding + horizontal border width.

The main advantage is predictable sizing: you can set, for example, width: 100% on an element without worrying that padding and borders will push it past its intended size.

question: What does !important mean in CSS?
What is specificity? How is specificity calculated?
answer:
Specificity is the algorithm the browser uses to decide which CSS rule applies to an element when multiple rules match. It determines which rule takes precedence.

Inline styles win over selectors in stylesheets. ID selectors win over class, attribute, and pseudo-class selectors, which in turn win over element (type) and pseudo-element selectors. The universal selector (*) and combinators have no specificity of their own. When two selectors have the same specificity, the one that appears later in the source order wins.

question: What is progressive rendering? How is it implemented, and what are its advantages?
What are the advantages of using translate() instead of absolute positioning?
answer: translate() does not trigger the browser's layout or paint steps; it runs on the compositor, which the GPU can handle very efficiently. Changing an element's position with absolute positioning triggers layout (reflow) and paint. As a result, translate() generally gives better performance, especially during animations.

question: Does style1.css have to be downloaded and parsed before style2.css can be fetched?
answer:
<head>

<link href="style1.css" rel="stylesheet">
<link href="style2.css" rel="stylesheet">

</head>

No. The browser fetches the stylesheets in the order they appear in the HTML, but the downloads happen in parallel. The browser does not need to finish downloading or parsing style1.css before requesting style2.css.

question: How can you determine whether the browser supports a particular CSS feature?
